topic: A student receives a much lower grade than expected
raw_output:
[story 1]

The red ink on the paper felt like a physical burn against my fingertips. A C-minus. It was a catastrophic blow to the meticulous planning I had put into this assignment. Across the desk, Sarah was already tapping her pen against her notebook, a small, satisfied smile playing on her lips as she reviewed her own excellent score. I stared at the grade, my jaw tightening until a dull ache radiated through my temples. I could feel a strange, hot clenching in my chest, a tight knot that refused to loosen. *Why her?* The thought echoed loudly in the sudden silence of my room. I picked up my pencil, the lead suddenly feeling heavy and useless, and began furiously scribbling meaningless doodles in the margin of my textbook, trying to channel the sudden, sharp irritation into something tangible.

[story 2]

"Honestly, I don't know what they're looking for," I muttered, pushing my chair back with a screech that made my stomach clench. The feedback sheet was stark, the lower mark mocking my efforts. I watched as Mark, who had breezed through the material, casually leaned back in his seat, a relaxed sigh escaping him. His ease was infuriating. Every time he spoke about his easy comprehension, a bitter taste rose in my mouth. I picked up my bag, the strap digging into my shoulder, and began pacing the length of my dorm room. My movements became jerky, my breathing shallow. I kept glancing towards Mark, cataloging the slight tilt of his head, the confident set of his shoulders, wishing I could somehow siphon off that effortless success.

[story 3]

My reflection in the darkened computer screen showed a pale, drawn face. The grade stared back, a verdict I hadn't earned. A slow, simmering resentment began to bubble up, hot and corrosive. I slammed my textbook shut, the sound sharp and disproportionate. I wanted to shout, to demand an explanation, but the words caught in my throat, replaced by a tense stillness. I watched as the other students clustered together, their easy laughter sounding like crystal bells compared to the heavy silence settling around me. I focused intensely on the texture of the wood grain on my desk, trying to anchor myself against the unsettling feeling that I was somehow, inexplicably, falling behind everyone else.
